figures.caption: Fig. 1. PSNR comparison of models pretrained only on ICVL [10] and the same backbones further finetuned with HIR-ALIGN, evaluated on CAVE [11]. X (Y) denotes the performance of model Y on task X.

Fig. 3. Details of the warp based spectral transfer process. We first match the generated and proxy RGB images using multi feature patch descriptors and neighbor retrieval. We then optimize soft aggregation weights and local interpolation kernels guided by RGB alignment losses. Finally we apply these shared learned parameters directly to the proxy hyperspectral data to synthesize the target aligned output.

TABLE I PERFORMANCE ON THE GAUSSIAN DENOISING TASK ON CAVE AND KAIST. THE COMPARISON COVERS AN UNSUPERVISED DIFFUSION BASELINE, CLASSICAL BASELINES, ICVL-PRETRAINED SUPERVISED MODELS, AND THE CORRESPONDING HIR-ALIGN-ADAPTED VARIANTS. THE BEST AND SECOND-BEST RESULTS ARE SHOWN IN BOLD AND UNDERLINED, RESPECTIVELY.

TABLE II PERFORMANCE ON THE COMPLEX DENOISING TASK ON CAVE AND KAIST. WE COMPARE CLASSICAL BASELINES, ICVL-PRETRAINED SUPERVISED MODELS, AND THE CORRESPONDING HIR-ALIGN-ADAPTED VARIANTS. THE BEST AND SECOND-BEST RESULTS ARE SHOWN IN BOLD AND UNDERLINED, RESPECTIVELY.

TABLE IV COMPREHENSIVE COMPARISON BETWEEN MODELS TRAINED ONLY ON ICVL AND THOSE PRETRAINED ON ICVL AND SUBSEQUENTLY ADAPTED WITH HIR-ALIGN. WE COMPARE MP-HSIR [21] AND PROMPTIR [52] ON CAVE AND KAIST ACROSS SIX HSI RESTORATION TASKS. THE FIRST THREE TASK GROUPS CORRESPOND TO GAUSSIAN DENOISING, COMPLEX DENOISING, AND SUPER-RESOLUTION, FOLLOWED BY DEBLURRING, BAND COMPLETION, AND INPAINTING. THE BEST RESULTS ARE HIGHLIGHTED IN BOLD.

TABLE V DENOISING PERFORMANCE ON HSIDWRD. WE COMPARE MODELS TRAINED ONLY ON ICVL AND THOSE FURTHER FINE-TUNED ON OUR SYNTHETIC DATASET. THE BEST AND SECOND-BEST RESULTS ARE SHOWN IN BOLD AND UNDERLINED, RESPECTIVELY.

Fig. 4. t-SNE visualizations of (a) CLIP features derived from RGB images and (b) spectra for degraded, proxy, synthesized, and GT samples. The synthesized samples move toward the target distribution in both spatial and spectral spaces while preserving diversity.

Fig. 2. Overview of HIR-ALIGN. (a) Proxy generation: source-pretrained HSI restorers produce proxy HSIs and proxy RGBs from degraded target observations. (b) Distribution-adaptive synthesis: improved unCLIP generates target-aligned RGBs, and the proposed pixel-matching spectral transfer module maps proxy spectra to the generated RGB layout by extracting pixel features from patch descriptors, retrieving candidates via neighborhood lookup, and applying soft aggregation with shared local interpolation. (c) Aligned supervised finetuning: re-degraded proxy pairs and synthesized HSI pairs are jointly used to finetune off-the-shelf restoration networks.
